methodsAttempted.query: site:msu.edu Michigan State University generative AI policy students academic integrity teaching guidance
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search found the central MSU AI Guidelines page, CTLI instructional guidance, Ethics Institute student guidance, and older Provost guidance.

methodsAttempted.query: site:msu.edu Michigan State University approved generative AI tools MSU IT research guidance data security
methodsAttempted.notes: Firecrawl search found MSU IT AI Security & Privacy standards, the MSU IT AI Tools page, research-office resources, communications standards, and a unit-specific Extension PDF.
The central AI Guidelines page states that the 2025 guidelines supersede previously issued AI guidance and serve as MSU's official framework.

robots.txt for ai.msu.edu allowed crawling and advertised the sitemap. teachingcenter.msu.edu and ethics.msu.edu also allowed crawling. tech.msu.edu robots.txt returned an Incapsula interstitial to plain curl, but Firecrawl fetched the public AI pages successfully.

artifacts.summary: Official-source-only discovery found MSU's central 2025 generative AI guidelines and supporting official pages from MSU IT, CTLI, and the MSU Ethics Institute. Older Provost pages were rejected for claim use because the central guidelines state that they supersede prior AI guidance. A unit-specific MSU Extension PDF was rejected for university-wide claims because it is scoped to Extension rather than central MSU policy.

artifacts.claimText: Michigan State University's 2025 Guidelines for the Use of Generative Artificial Intelligence serve as MSU's official framework for ethical, responsible, and equitable generative AI use and supersede previously issued AI guidance.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: MSU guidelines say students may use generative AI tools for coursework or research activities only when the instructor or research advisor explicitly permits that use.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: MSU expects instructors to include course-specific generative AI guidance in every syllabus, including whether AI use is permitted, the contexts in which it may be used, and expected acknowledgment or citation practices.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.claimText: MSU guidelines generally discourage generative AI detection tools and say detection outputs should be treated as potential indicators rather than conclusive evidence or the sole basis for academic or grading decisions.

artifacts.claimText: MSU guidelines say third-party generative AI tools, especially those operated outside the United States, may be used only with non-sensitive public information unless MSU IT Information Security gives prior approval.

artifacts.claimText: MSU IT standards say only AI platforms formally evaluated and recommended by MSU IT Governance, Risk, and Compliance may be used with institutional data, and no AI tool should be assumed safe for confidential or regulated data unless explicitly approved.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines | AI | Michigan State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: These guidelines supersede all previously issued guidance related to the use of generative AI tools at MSU. They supplement existing university policies, standards, and procedures, and serve as the university’s official framework for the ethical, responsible, and equitable use of generative AI.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines | AI | Michigan State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Students may only use generative AI tools to support their coursework or research activities when explicitly permitted by the instructor/research advisor.
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines | AI | Michigan State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The university expects instructors to include generative AI course guidance with a clear statement in every syllabus. This statement should specify whether generative AI use is permitted, the contexts in which it may be used (e.g., assignments, exams, collaborative projects), and how students are expected to appropriately acknowledge and cite their use of generative AI applications.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Permissible Uses - Teaching and Learning - Educators
artifacts.rightsNote: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

citation.sourceRights: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines | AI | Michigan State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: The use of generative AI detection tools is generally discouraged. However, if an instructor chooses to use such tools, they must clearly inform students about their intended use, including the rationale, how results will be interpreted, and what actions may follow. Detection tool outputs should be considered potential indicators—not conclusive evidence—of generative AI misuse and should never serve as the sole basis for academic or grading decisions.
artifacts.evidenceLocator: Permissible Uses - Teaching and Learning - Educators

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines | AI | Michigan State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Third-party generative AI tools, particularly those operated outside the United States, pose significant risks to data security and intellectual property. These tools may only be used with non-sensitive, public information unless prior approval is obtained from MSU IT Information Security.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Guidelines | AI | Michigan State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Integration of generative AI into research must be disclosed in research outputs, manuscripts, artistic endeavors, and grant applications in accordance with the guidance/policies and expectations of publishers, funders, and collaborators. This may include idea generation, data analysis, and drafting. In the absence of stated guidance/policy, researchers are expected to disclose any intentional and substantial uses of AI.

artifacts.sourceTitle: Student | Ethics | Michigan State University
artifacts.evidenceSnippetOriginal: Please follow the policies provided by your instructors or course syllabuses before using AI on assignments or in your classes. ... Communicate clearly and specifically to instructors and classmates if/when you use AI for assignments, brainstorming, etc. per the instructors’ guidance and class expectations. ... When in doubt, ask! Check with your instructors to ensure that your use of generative AI aligns with what is acceptable for your class or project.

artifacts.notes: Approved because the claim directly tracks the central MSU AI Guidelines page and does not publish the staged candidate as canonical.
Approved because the source explicitly says student use is permitted only with instructor or research-advisor permission.
Approved because the claim is narrowly scoped to instructor syllabus/course guidance expectations stated in the central guidelines.
Approved because the source explicitly discourages detection tools and limits evidentiary use of detector outputs.
Approved because the source states third-party generative AI tools may only be used with non-sensitive public information unless MSU IT Information Security approves otherwise.
Approved because the MSU IT source directly states the GRC evaluation and approval requirement for institutional and regulated data use.
Approved because the MSU IT tools page directly states the enterprise security and data-protection framing for MSU enterprise-licensed AI tools.
Approved because the MSU IT tools page directly states that non-enterprise AI purchases require IT Readiness form completion and approval.
Approved because the central guidelines directly state disclosure expectations for generative AI integration in research outputs and grant applications.
Approved because the Ethics Institute student guidance directly supports the claim, while the claim remains scoped to student guidance rather than central policy.

artifacts.title: Michigan State University Generative AI Guidance Staged Review
artifacts.summary: Staged MSU artifacts identify the 2025 central AI Guidelines as the current official framework and extract narrow claims on student permission, course-specific syllabus guidance, detector-output limits, third-party data restrictions, MSU IT security review, enterprise AI tools, non-enterprise AI procurement, research disclosure, and student transparency guidance.
limitations: This tracker is not legal advice, not academic integrity advice, and not an official university statement unless a linked source is the university's own official page.
The staged claims are not canonical public claims and have not been human reviewed.
Older 2023 Provost guidance was not used for current claims because MSU's 2025 guidelines state that they supersede previous AI guidance.
The MSU Extension PDF was not used for university-wide claims because it is unit-specific.
artifacts.sourceRightsPolicy: Tracker metadata is open licensed. Official source documents, page text, PDFs, and other source materials retain their original rights and terms.
